skill.name: burp-suite-testing
skill.description: Guide Burp Suite workflows for authorized web application security testing, including proxy setup, request replay, scanning, and manual vulnerability checks.
skill.summary: Run authorized Burp Suite testing workflows for web application security reviews.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, filesystem, password file, and weak cryptography patterns, but the reviewed evidence is Markdown guidance and payload documentation rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious host-side behavior was found. The skill remains medium risk because it provides dual-use Burp Suite exploitation workflows, Intruder attack guidance, and common attack payloads that require strict authorization.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Web Exploitation Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches Burp Suite workflows for vulnerability discovery and exploitation, including request modification, proof-of-concept exploits, SQL injection, and authentication bypass examples. This is legitimate for authorized testing, but it can enable misuse against systems without permission.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines explicitly describe exploitation-oriented Burp Suite testing and proof-of-concept outputs. The skill also includes authorization guardrails, so the concern is dual-use risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Automated Intruder Attack Guidance
medium_findings.description: The Intruder section explains configuring attack types and payload sets, including credential testing examples. These workflows can support authorized fuzzing, but they can also support brute-force or credential attack activity if misused.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence directly covers Intruder attack configuration and sample username and password payloads. Guardrails at lines 305-307 reduce but do not remove misuse potential.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive File and Command Injection Payload Examples
medium_findings.description: The quick reference lists path traversal and command injection payloads, including password file paths and shell command examples. They are presented as testing payloads, not executed by the skill, but they are still dual-use attack material.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly contain path traversal, password file, and command injection payload strings. Context shows documentation for Burp testing rather than local command execution.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Hits Are Markdown Syntax
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in Markdown code fences, inline formatting, HTTP examples, and payload examples. No executable script invokes these commands from the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited locations are inside SKILL.md documentation and formatting. I found no evidence of runnable Ruby, shell backtick execution, or command dispatch logic.

low_findings.title: Local Burp Proxy Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP and URL findings refer to Burp Suite local proxy setup and the local certificate download page. They do not identify outbound exfiltration or unauthorized remote network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The IP address is 127.0.0.1 for a local proxy listener, and http://burp is the standard Burp local certificate endpoint. No external callback or data transfer logic appears in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography static hits align with ordinary prose and table text, not with cryptographic API usage. No MD5, DES, SHA-1, or similar implementation was found in the reviewed skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata, headings, tables, or prose. I found no evidence of cryptographic operations or instructions to use weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: System Password File Access Is Documentation Only
low_findings.description: The password file strings are included as web security test payloads for path traversal and command injection checks. The skill does not read local system files or instruct the AI agent to access host files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The strings appear in a common testing payload list under Quick Reference. There is no surrounding code that opens, reads, or transmits local password files.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are Contextual
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hits are references to credentials, test parameters, rate limits, and a command injection payload. They do not instruct the agent to enumerate the local host or collect system data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are prerequisites, Burp test examples, or safety guidance. Only line 293 contains a reconnaissance command string, and it appears inside a web command injection payload list.

dangerous_patterns.title: Path Traversal Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes Unix and Windows path traversal payload examples for web application testing. These are dangerous when used outside authorized assessment scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payloads are explicit and commonly used to test file disclosure flaws. They are documented as testing strings rather than executed locally.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Injection Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill lists shell metacharacter payloads and a whoami example for command injection testing. This content is dual-use and should be restricted to permitted targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is an explicit command injection payload block. Context supports authorized testing, but the payloads are inherently sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Attack Payload Setup
dangerous_patterns.description: The Intruder payload section demonstrates username and password candidate sets. This can be valid for authorized testing but can also support password guessing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payload sets include common usernames and passwords in an Intruder attack workflow. The evidence is direct, with operational misuse possible.

content.user_title: Test Web Apps with Burp Suite
content.value_statement: Web application testing can miss issues when traffic is not inspected closely. This skill guides authorized Burp Suite workflows for interception, replay, scanning, and focused manual checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to launch Burp Suite and configure a browser proxy.
Guides HTTP interception, request modification, and forwarding workflows.
Shows how to define target scope and filter HTTP history.
Describes Repeater workflows for manual parameter testing.
Outlines Professional Edition scan setup and review steps.
Summarizes Intruder attack types and result analysis methods.
limitations: Requires Burp Suite and a browser configured for proxy testing.
Automated scanning guidance depends on Burp Suite Professional Edition.
Does not verify that the user has authorization for a target.
Results still require human validation to remove false positives.

use_cases.title: Validate Access Control
use_cases.description: Use intercepted requests to test whether user identifiers or hidden fields can be modified to reach unauthorized data.

use_cases.title: Review Authenticated Traffic
use_cases.description: Capture logged-in application flows, define target scope, and document relevant requests for security review.

use_cases.title: Triage Scanner Findings
use_cases.description: Review Burp issue details, replay requests, and compare responses before writing remediation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Burp Proxy
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an authorized target.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me configure Burp Suite and a browser to intercept traffic for my authorized test target.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Captured Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Review a request before manual testing.
prompt_templates.prompt: I captured an HTTP request in Burp. Help me identify parameters worth testing and explain the safest test order.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Repeater Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Manual validation of a suspected vulnerability.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Burp Repeater test plan for this authorized endpoint, including inputs to try and response signals to record.

prompt_templates.title: Build Assessment Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an authorized Burp Suite assessment workflow covering scope, proxy capture, Repeater validation, scanning, Intruder limits, and reporting.

output_examples.input: User asks how to begin testing an authorized staging site with Burp Suite.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step setup plan covering project creation, proxy listener checks, browser configuration, certificate installation, and scope definition.

output_examples.input: User provides a captured request and asks what to test next.
output_examples.output: A prioritized checklist of parameters, expected safe test cases, response indicators, and notes to include in the final report.

output_examples.input: User wants to validate a scanner issue before reporting it.
output_examples.output: A manual verification workflow using issue details, Repeater comparisons, evidence capture, false-positive checks, and remediation notes.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization and scope before any testing begins.
Use Burp target scope and rate limits to avoid unintended traffic.
Manually validate automated findings before reporting them.
anti_patterns: Do not test systems without explicit permission.
Do not run broad Intruder attacks without scope and rate controls.
Do not treat scanner output as confirmed evidence without validation.

faq.question: Does this skill require Burp Suite Professional?
faq.answer: No. Proxy, Repeater, and limited Intruder workflows work in Community Edition. Automated scanning requires Professional Edition.

faq.question: Can this skill perform testing by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance. The user must run Burp Suite and control testing against authorized targets.

faq.question: Is this safe for production systems?
faq.answer: Only with explicit approval, defined scope, and rate limits. Some tests can change data or affect availability.

faq.question: Does it include reporting guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It points users toward documenting requests, responses, findings, and remediation advice.

faq.question: What are the main risks?
faq.answer: The main risks are out-of-scope testing, excessive scan traffic, and misuse of dual-use payloads.

faq.question: Can it validate false positives?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to replay requests, compare responses, and review evidence before reporting an issue.
